title: 13/11/2025 - PP
displayTitle: 2025-11-12 - Primary Production Committee - Briefing on Government support for research and development in the primary industries
topic: Briefing on Government support for research and development in the primary industries
description: Briefing on Government support for research and development in the primary industries Hearing of evidence Bloomz New Zealand Andy Warren, Managing Director Louisa van Den Berg, Client Manager Chris Claridge, Yummy Fruit Paul Paynter, General Manager Briefing on the yellow-legged hornet incursion Hearing of evidence Ministry for Primary industries Mike Inglis, Commissioner North Andrew Spelman, Commissioner Biosecurity, Intel & Response
descriptionSummary: Briefing on Government support for research and development in the primary industries. Bloomz New Zealand. Andy Warren, Managing Director

chunks.text: Minutes. I use that time's up to you, but if you'd like questions, I suggest you, um, give yourself some time at the end for those questions. You, you have my submission in front of you? Yes, we do. So I'll go through it reasonably quickly. Brilliant. With a few war stories so that, uh, you get a bit of an idea where we're at. Yeah. Yeah. Good morning.

Thanks for the opportunity for coming, uh, for us to be here. I'm Andy Warren from Bloom, New Zealand Limited, and I have got my colleagues, Louisa Vandenberg, who I've worked with for 25 years, been right around the world on many a mission and Chris Claridge, who is well known on the horticultural industry. We also represent the New Zealand Fruit Tree Importers Group.

We are the convener of that, which is a group of corporates who are the key importers in New Zealand. There's only about seven importers in the actual whole live plant industry. So we've been actively campaigning for complete overhaul of the light plant import regulations and system for about 10 years.

So what we ask here today, and the key questions are an independent review in order to the proposed plant health environment capability facility, and the associated plant import process to be co governed by the actual users of the facilities, which is us as importers.

Secondly, an examination of an alternative business model for live plant importing and diagnostic testing. And the third thing is the import processes designed, monitored, and managed in collaboration with actual importers as opposed to anybody else. So who are we? We, I've been personally importing and exporting live plants for over 40 years, and during that time, I've witnessed the degradation of NPI services more recently due to an erosion of technical capability and capacity, and a very high turnover of staff demonstrating a significant loss of institutional knowledge.

And that's a pretty critical issue. We've got support today from key drives, including 85% of the, at least 85% of the Apple industry exports, including New Zealand Fruit Tree Company, the largest importer in New Zealand, the Yummy Fruit Company, poor painter, market gardeners, fresh Max Fruit Craft, that's the Bostock Group, Mr.

Apple Craigmore Investments, FreshCo Southern Produce, New Zealand, China Fruit Company, and the largest citrus operator in New Zealand who do about 50% of export first fresh. We've also the support of the actual facilitator of NPIs import reform workshops, professor Hamish Gar from Lincoln University.

Our concerns, we have 12 key points that we wish to present. Even one of them would be enough to suggest to MPI that we need to review and look at this whole process. First point is imports are currently largely frozen, and, uh, we note that 85% of new plant right variety rights are from offshore breeding.

So the importance of in import cannot be underestimated. It's simply unaffordable. Two, less than 50% of the available MPIL three B post entry quarantine space is currently being utilized. And that's likely to go down as we use up the old, uh, charging areas that may get down to as low as 30% three NPIs plant quarantine facility charges are at least three times more than any other equivalent facility around the world.

Four over 1000 import health standards have just been suspended. MPI had not completed one new live plant import health standard in the past 18 months, despite many requests, we still don't have an import health standard for pears, apple, and pears for over 26 years, despite many requests.

And the root stock that goes with that. This is a huge issue for our premier apple and peer breeding organization. Prevar up in Hawkes Bay. Paul will talk about that five. The IHS review, that's the import health standard review process, needs review and radical change. I think MPI actually agree with that.

Given a thousand standards have been suspended, and it's taking MPI somewhere between 8,000 and 15,000 man hours or woman hours to do one live plant in import health standard, we've gotta look at a change. And that's why in 0.6, we proposed the use of AI using an ontological database, uh, arrangement.

This has already been done in Australia, and that's why we're suggesting we need to look at other countries to do that rather than trying to develop it ourselves. Seven, there's insufficient recognition of offshore clean plant facilities. There's about eight, which are the source of most of the new varieties for new crops in New Zealand.

Ke fruit, apples, stone fruit, citrus, avocados, all that kind of thing, and all the testing that's done by them offshore. So the point is to get it done before it even gets here. For example, nack tobo in Netherlands has only three of the 30 tests required for prunus recognized by MPI.

So there's consequently massive duplication and a big cost to industry, something Mr. Hoard was quite concerned about. So number eight, there is no evidence of growth in diagnostic testing demand and like, and unlikely to be any increase in surveillance testing unless more budget is put together for associated.

So, uh, surveillance programs, we see none of that at this point. Nine MPI have spent over the last five years, $102 million on planning, designing and developing a business case for the PHCC project in Mount Albert and projecting a total cost of between 500 million and a billion dollars.

By the way, there's been four separate design efforts on that already. So we are, we are really concerned about the institutional knowledge issue there. We would just keep on going round and round 10. The business case for PHCC was written without industry collaboration and the projected cost is up to a hundred times more than a privately designed testing facility with cutting edge testing and diagnostic capability.

11. There, there's been an exponential change, and I mean, a totally rapid change in testing and PE deduction methodologies in the last five or six years since that 2019 start to that project. So things have changed markedly. We've been through COVID, um, and this is eclipsed their approach to risk management to the extent that NPIs testing charges now are in excess of 15 times, and I say 15 times what a private facility would charge.

And we've put together a very, um, complex appendix, um, which Mr. Claridge can speak to. We've provided a full cost of testing model by a third party and a direct comparison using cherries as an example. So in the case of cherries, MPI $20,000 and now new approach academically endorse and biosecurity security, Safeway $1,500.

So you can see there's a massive change in costs that we've come up with. And 12 as recently of last month, NPR are proposing to increase the cost of their services, 300% increase to certification fees alone due to a historic deficit. So therefore, to be blunt, we have no confidence in NPIs ability to restrain costs over time.

The questions that need answering why has MPI ignored the 12 points we have raised. They cannot be unaware of them. The formal live plant import pathway have actually proved incredibly safe. We haven't had an in, uh, an incursion in 30 years.

And the big risks right now, and you'll hear it in the paper all the time, are from freight, e-commerce, passengers, and dare I say, it's smuggling. And we can prove that we've notified NPI on numerous times about potential smuggling because when things get too expensive and too difficult, people tend to put things in their gumbo and bring them in.

And we can quote you that we know about it. So why aren't these a priority? Why have MPI failed to engage with users in the consultation process. When we went up there one time, they thought that the scientists at PHEL were the users, not actually industry. So there's a bit of an issue there.

So the recent import reform workshops are an example of NPIs poor engagement with stakeholders. The issues are already in well known with similar exercises conducted repeatedly over the last 10 years. So why we asked, do we not build a collaborative model of testing with other providers?

So Andy, you're coming up to nine minutes now. You want, you're coming up to nine minutes. Yep. No, I'm of your I'm in time. Yeah. So testing and diagnostics, there have been already exponential changes in methodologies. Gene sequencing is no longer limited to viruses, and we can do a lot of other things.

So we have no confidence in the outdated design of PHEC. There are at least seven companies in China producing these new machines that do this job. And this has all come out of COVID testing where COVID tests now have got down to as low as 15 cents. So you're testing a virus, same as a human virus.

It's pretty cheap. So the volume of testing done by MPI hasn't changed in five years. It's still, when we've done an analysis of this a hundred thousand tests a year, that has not changed. So we're quite concerned that they're reporting, oh, we need a lot of surge capacity and all this sort of thing. The actual figures don't represent that a medium sized gene sequencing machine can do 500,000 tests a year, and you're talking of less than $20 million for that specific machine.

So, such testing could be done by a private provider in New Zealand that 15 times cheaper than what MPI is currently charging. For example, we've just had something go through, we were charged $850 plus GST per sample for cherry testing in our model, we can do the same testing for $35.

And this has largely been enabled by the explosion of cheap testing, as I suggested before from China post COVID. There are other tools that can help detect and identify high priority pests. These are being regularly used overseas, especially in the Netherlands, the world's second largest agricultural exporter and a lot of other countries.

So in conclusion, we've now reached a view that MPI has an agenda of maintaining a monopolistic control of plant imports and testing under the guise of biosecurity, regardless of the evidence presented. MPIs, the regulator, funder, and monopoly service provider for the HT sector industry has no mechanism to hold NPI to account apart from us in front of literally your select committee today.

So we conclude that the PHCC project in Auckland is not commercially viable. And NPI now in serious danger of developing a facility, which will be redundant by the time it opens in 29 30, look at ville that was three years behind and 30% over budget. So, um, and we see it as irresponsible and a significant waste of taxpayer funds in a time of fiscal restraint.

So in short, the passage of time since inception, it could probably only be described as a vanity project. People tend to hold onto things. The facility itself will not increase exports. We propose a pause and proceedings like the Cook State Fer project to re-scope the project in consideration of an alternative public private partnership with a revamp laboratory plan and PEQ facility that, that uses established offshore design and builds a system, not like the bespoke system that was used in the interim arrangement,

which quite frankly is not too good to look at. I'm gonna, I'm gonna stop you there and go to questions because we're running outta time. So Mike, Um, thanks Jeff. Um, just a question for you, Annie. What's the impact of the delays, the suspensions, um, of your import health standards? What's that mean? Well, You, you could probably measure the impact in billions of dollars to the extent that new varieties are not coming in, and Paul Painter is gonna talk a lot about that on the effect of actual, um, for growers and marketers in the industry.

So you're talking of billions of dollars, us, Sam, Sam, Steve, then Mark, I note, note your point about, um, the Fed project not being commercially viable and being very expensive and potentially irrelevant. Um, so thank you for sharing those thoughts, but just wanted to ask you, who are the stakeholders of the Fed project and how much engagement has there been with bi?

Okay. The actual stakeholders are the importers. There's about nine sectors in agriculture, um, for high value, starting with apples, stone fruit, citrus, avocados, all the berry crops, potatoes, that kind of thing. And those are the stakeholders.

There's actually about seven regular importers. So they're the stakeholders, not PHEL. Okay. Yep. How much engagement does it and how much engagement we've had very little. We've had two one hour teams meetings, and they did ask Lloyd and I to go and have a review of their interim facility and we wrote them a report and we went to impress it was non-sustainable. Right.

Yeah. Could I ask a, so very close up. And, and on the back of that, those two hours, those teams meetings, I assume you gave them similar feedback to what she was today. Absolutely. We, we fed this back regularly and, um, I, I know that Stuart Anderson wrote you a letter, um, here, um, and which was in response to questions posed by us to this committee.

Thanks, Steve. And the points we've raised are absolutely factual backed by data and figures such as the alternative pricing model that we put in. Yes. Appendix one. Yep. Yep. Alright, Steve, so is there a risk that here we have a facility that we're, we've been locked into the PHEC facility that is now 'cause of the evolution of technology, as you say, redundant, and that we as a parliament need to reevaluate the wisdom of that investment?

I think you've hit the nail on the head. I understand. The point is there's been a massive exponential change in testing methodology since 2019 or so when this whole thing was moed. Uh, Chris could possibly comment on that. So Gene sequencing has rapidly evolved since COVID testing, uh, the seven suppliers of the equipment out of China.

Currently, NPI is sending samples to China to undertake gene sequencing. There is gene sequencing machine sitting on LIC Fonterra AG Research and Lincoln, there's sitting idle faster. That gene sequencing machine in Lincoln University was half a million dollars. It could run all of the tests.

100,000 tests using five staff now. So something's been disconnected. The suppliers of the equipment have not been made contact by NPI. No one in the industry has had any engagement through any of the business cases. So everyone's looking at this slightly scans going, what has happened here?

It's a disconnect. Yeah. And the sub would be, um, is it a problem of the inertia of the ministry that they're locked into this project instead of hell bent on completing it? And, and so there's, it's very difficult for them to hear your message that it's already outta date. Absence of capability of capacity of the understanding of technology management at the highest levels, high tune of technical staff, inability to retain skilled staff.

Yep. So you've got and four separate business cases, none of which we've been able to get hold of. We don't know actually what they've done in their analysis and the scope of it. Yeah. That's why we're saying stop. Let's go back and have another look at this before we spend a billion dollars because we could build a hundred of these facilities in New Zealand to give you some idea of the disconnect given the new technology, the experts in New Zealand on technology testing machines have not been consulted by NPI.

Okay. We've got a final question from Mark. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, um, Andrew, good to see you online. Um, and apologies for my absence. Your detractors, sir. Your detractors would turn around and say that you are one of the larger players in the industry, and by virtue you stand to gain the best, um, advantage as it were.

Um, in terms of reduction of levies and costs to your enterprise. How do you square that, given that you are being very, very, um, objectively critical of MPI? Because that's what people will say. How do you square that, Mark? Thanks for your question. Good question.

I think the facts are, we are a facilitator only. So in the actual, uh, benefit that we make out of it is quite minimal compared with the benefit to industry. We are just simply the messenger, the organ through which this goes. And on that basis, pulling together, uh, this whole process, somebody had to do it.

And given the establishment of our fruit tree importers group, um, over the last five to six years, that's how we've done it. And they've asked us to stand there for them. I I can add another point to that. The key issue here is to lower the barrier to entry. Mm-hmm. If, if Blooms is looking to secure, uh, a monopoly situation, they'd encourage the barrier to go up, not down.

It would, it's so that argument doesn't wash. Yeah. And it's an extremely complex, uh, effort to bring in material. We've done it for 10 years or so. Mm-hmm. And there's probably not room given the fact there's only seven importers, so for a whole heap of other companies to be in there.

So our benefit is much more simply as a facilitator. Have we got time for supplementary Mr? No, We're well over time. Sorry, mark. Uh, so thanks very much for your, um, submission today. Uh, uh, Andy and team, um, we're gonna move on now to, uh, Paul Berry and, um, his submissions.

But you're more than welcome to stay here and listen anyway. Yeah, we have numerous appendices and worksheets. Any other information should you require that? Thank you. We've done a lot of research. Thank You. So thanks for taking the, making the effort. Thank you. No, I appreciate the time.

So, uh, welcome Paul. Um, we'll take it as that we've read your submission. Um, you have, uh, 15 minutes, uh, you use, that's up to you. But I would suggest you leave some time for questions because the committee's obviously quite interested.

So, uh, far away. Thank you. Appreciate your time today. And I know this is the engine room of parliament here, and, uh, this is a great committee. We sort of stalked you from afar and, uh, and, uh, uh, and the rural community and this, and we think this make this team's very good. Um, so in fact, the rural community's favorite green mp, um, and probably the rural community's favorite party Maori, um, uh, it's, um, the, uh, so I'm, I suppose I'm here to give a commercial perspective.

Yes. And, uh, it's important to understand there are a lot of different biases based on where you are on the chain. If you're a, a bureaucrat or even an industry body, including our industry body, the rights biosecurity after that, they put protect. But the opportunity for plant to put pathways is, uh, under thought about by NPI and the industry bodies because they can see the downsides of getting something wrong.

They can't see the opportunity to getting it right. And that's particularly because of the lag. Uh, quite oftentimes when we're reporting material, I see a great nectarine in California now that'll take me 10 years, uh, to be in commercial production if I move irresponsibly quickly. If you, uh, if you test it a bit better and we take, we're at the moment, we are three years offshore quarantine, one year onshore, and then we get a couple little sticks.

We have to bulk up the material, plant some test trees. Most people would like to have a look at that for a few years. And so, uh, then the planted orchard, you five years into production, your real benefits, uh, once you get the material, uh, probably in the 10 to 20 year bracket. And, uh, and you could probably add 10 years in, in testing and bulking up, uh, and getting the, the propagation right.

And there's probably 10 years in terms of the importation and, uh, and getting that right. So it's a very, very long process from beginning to end. Uh, so that takes any bias of it because anything we bring in today, I'll be gone by the time we are making money out of it. So it really is about providing opportunity for the future. Uh, and whatever decisions you make will have a big impact on that.

And I wanna remember your name, but it's important you get this right. Um, so our business has been over its whole history based on plant imports. We have no, uh, credible domestic stone fruit breeding program. Well, I would say we are not providing leadership at any breeding program whatsoever. Um, probably kiwi fruit and apples are in the leading echelon, but, uh, certainly don't have the leadership they probably had 20 years ago.

And there are a lot of industries that simply have nothing. So there's huge amount of opportunity to bring material in because New Zealand has to be in that premium space, and we have to be presenting consumers, which increasingly are Asian consumers with products that really wow them. And we are capable of doing that. But we need the germ plasm perhaps, to start breeding programs.

Uh, and we need to do good varieties of the material that's gonna achieve those premium prices. Uh, we're a commercial company, but we invest in, uh, two plant intellectual property, uh, companies. We have a shareholding in them. Uh, and we invest either directly or indirectly into three breeding programs, including one in France, of which I'm a director.

And, uh, and that's producing red fleshed apples, and they're gonna change the world. Uh, and we're really excited about it, but it's a slow pathway getting them in and getting 'em up and running and everybody else is making progress and, and we are not. So I guess in terms of getting the plant import pathways, right, the best example we have of a similar country that we share a lot of similar biosecurity values with is Australia at Lum, which I think opened in 2015.

They went through about a decade long process to develop their new quarantine system. Uh, and it's basically the growers there are smiling from ear to ear, uh, because they've got a one year system of bringing material in, and they're certainly adopting more progressive approach to technology. And that's what's really changing some of the, uh, new gene sequencing technologies.

You know, the human genome took 10 years to map, and the machinery now can do it in a day. So the technology's moving extremely fast. I had a quick review of the literature on, um, virus testing technology, uh, in the last few days. And, uh, you know, in the last five years, the amount of progress being made is enormous.

And so it's very important. I think I've very rudely said in my thing that the, it looks to me like NPIA building a tank in the age of drones. And that's how quickly things are are changing. And so we have to be, uh, pretty dynamic about it. But the number one thing we can't afford to have is the slowest and most expensive pathway for fire imports.

We are totally committed to biosecurity. I'm fearful every day that some tourist is gonna bring in brown rated stink bugger have a young son who's very enthusiastic beekeeper, and he, um, he's, he's super hot on the hornet tissue. Uh, doesn't think that necessarily was handled that well. We are very aware of the risks.

Uh, NP often say, oh, you growers don't care. I got more skin in the game than anybody else. So we are very focused on biosecurity. We have to get that right, but we have to balance that with the opportunity. And there are many more small sectors with the costs of importation. Also, the risk, uh, that material I bring out in California for stone fruit, well, I don't import it, but we, uh, we fund it probably we have about a 10% success rate of commercially uh, developing those varieties because California doesn't have bacteria,

poly pseudomonas doesn't have mono linea particular brown rock that we have different species. I dunno what the rain is. Uh, so, uh, when we bring it here, we find it doesn't perform in our condition. So there's a lot of things that get cast aside. Uh, we are probably bringing more in from France, and the primary reason from that is not better material, but higher levels of success because their climate's a little more similar.

So there are lots of industries that can't even contemplate bringing things in. In the very start, we did those import health standards because, um, uh, Andy mentioned peers as a new peer in, uh, in Europe with that trademarked Fred, which is a very strange trademark, probably works in Europe. It's actually quite a good peer. I'm very impressed what you can buy on the supermarket as patterns tri from the late 18 hundreds and bur Bos from the early 18 hundreds.

So it's so far off the, off the pace for 26 years, no new peers. And there are things happening around the world in that space. And so all of these minor industries can't afford the plant, uh, import pathways, and they're kind of languishing on old material. And so it's very important that we can, uh, put New Zealand at in that premium space.

That Asian market is very desirous of high quality produce, has a reverence for it. There's nothing like you see in Germany, the one fill of bags of apples for, but the Asian consumer, they'll have, they'll like a tradesmen in the craft beer aisle. They'll, they're the drill times's ridiculous. So we're looking at an apple five different directions and taking a long time to buy it, and they'll take it back and from the desk and people will pass by saying, this is a beautiful thing you have on there, a little discussion, very odd, uh, dynamic.

But boy, if the quality's 10% better over there, they'll pay you another $10 a box. It's 10% better in Europe, same price. So we've got a gold mine, uh, on our doorstep, and there's a lot of industries with counter seasonal, uh, supply opportunities, but they don't have the ger plasm to develop varieties here, all the new varieties themselves to take to that market.

So we have to have, uh, a part import pathway that takes advantage of the opportunity we need to lay down for future iterations. I went to Massey University there, uh, earth science and, uh, agriculture awards last year, and, um, I can tell you that the kids coming through are frighteningly good.

Um, about 90% of the wards were won by young women, and they had maturity and poise and intelligence, uh, that, uh, I sort of remember, um, them not speaking to people when I was 19. But, um, they're amazing. They're so far advanced. The future is these young women. And I tried to employ one of them, um, who I originally employed as a graduate, and she said, no, thanks very much.

29 years old, uh, being offered a six figure salary and went off to Australia. But the talents there and what we have to present is the opportunities. A lot of our sectors old, the growers are old. The material they're producing is not that exciting for the international market, but the potential's huge. But we have to get that part in pathway right, uh, at the right price.

And I could argue there's a public good, uh, value in, in, uh, assisting some industries to fund that pathway. Thanks very much, Paul. I I did appreciate your, um, written submission. There were a couple of bits in there that made be laugh, actually, particularly where you talked about, um, any, you know, any fool can do it, and you, um, then, uh, accounted yourself as being overqualified in that area, so mm-hmm.

Um, but we'll go straight to questions and Steve's, uh, Got some questions for You. Yeah, I mean, your analogy that we are boarding a tank, uh, when we're in the air drones, uh, seems a powerful one. What are the actual risks in terms of lowering the biosecurity standard if we have multiple facilities doing testing at a much lower cost and potentially therefore more, um, uh, live matter coming into the country.

Do do you think that's a, a thing, a question we need to be asking? Is that an anxiety, that biosecurity point of view? MPI would rightly have. Um, yes, in part, I, I'm not, there's, there's a couple of ways of handling that The offshore facilities, um, that I've visited have been very professional and obviously a larger scale and doing many more tests than ours.

So I think they're very good people. Um, couple of 'em have full and short and staff turnover and things, which NPIs enjoyed is, uh, what they've had at Prosser in the US as well. They had a big, we that scandal a few years ago where they couldn't find the data and they weren't sure they'd done the tests. Uh, that's really down to staff turnover, right? Um, and, uh, that place hasn't been all that well run, but it's also down to the fact we took about six years to find there was a problem.

Uh, there was a, a, a policy in there, uh, about annual desktop audits, and important to have low level of audits right through the system on a regular basis to ensure that your counterparts, if you are outsourcing, are doing the right job. But I think that you certainly can drive out costs when you go to facilities are doing tens of thousands of tests over a, over a handful.

So it's a matter of what, what's better to be done in New Zealand, and we definitely need a quarantine service here, uh, performing a range of services and what could be more efficiently outsourced to credible organizations that we can manage, uh, effectively from a far. And so I think there's a, there's a balance in there, but if I was NPI and, and I was on that career, I would like a new building.

I'd like all the toys and I'd like to do it myself, but, um, so it is does take a degree of humility to say, Hey, the cheaper deal might be for us not to have that capacity, but, but outsource it. Okay. Thank you. Go to Mike and then Mark. Uh, thanks Marc. I just had a question for you around the ham quarantine station and open in 2015.

Has it always been one year quarantine? No. From when it was open? So has That changed since? It changed, but that was a big change. They had a similar system to us. Yep. But they looked at their, they reviewed their risk. One of the smart things they did for a lot of products was they did a survey of pest and diseases and found out that half the things they were looking for had been in Australia for 120 years and no one had noticed.

Uh, so that shortened the pest list, uh, which was helpful. And then they incorporated technologies, and some of those technologies weren't as proven as they are now, but what they said was, if you've got a range of forest statistic technologies and you're only 99.8% sure, if you can layer some of those on top of each other, you get a confidence level that is, uh, is adequate.

So they've certainly taken a very progressive approach and continue to do so. And it's just a matter of, I mean, they've got some advantages. My partners at Montagues over there, uh, grilling from ear to ear, and they're gonna beat me to market, and I get a bit upset about that. Uh, but they, they certainly seem to have been reasonably successful, but they've laid a lot of mistakes, uh, in that facility.

And if we could look at what they've done in terms of process and find out with bit honesty what their mistakes have been, we can do even better than, than they have done. So I think there's, um, yeah, there's an opportunity to learn from what's happened offshore. Uh, Canada's gone through a process as well. There's been a number of countries do things. So a review of how they're incorporating technology or how they intend to incorporate it as it develops, uh, is, is pretty important.

Okay. Mark, Mr. Chair, thank you for so much, Paul. Good to see you. Um, trying to square, um, your remarks in and around NPI, um, and formally the remarks of Andy Warren. What do you think is going on in NPI Do you think they've got operational hubris and they don't listen too, the industry players?

Uh, do you think there's a case of that and, and if you are king for day, um, what do you think should be the way forward? The, the facility is disbanded and we privatize, um, the, the type of technology moving forward that would be required for biosecurity protocols to a greater degree.

Uh, I think any large centralized organization, uh, is inclined to a bit of hubris. Uh, and that's not confined to government entities. I think industry bodies SPR and now and again, uh, that can happen. Um, and it does take a lot of humility to, to break out of that for sure. Um, but yeah, I don't wanna be king for a day.

Uh, they've got a job to do, but it's just a matter of, of approaching it with humility and a very progressive mindset, and also connecting and engaging with the industries in terms of, uh, the opportunity provides them. And one of the challenges I've got is to start talking to h research and, uh, to, um, to, uh, HNZ and, and Apple peers and Sun Fruit New Zealand about this because the people we used to employ as CEOs and, and, uh, and you know, chairman things used to be, uh, industry people or former growers, and they now tend to be more, um, you know, career, uh,

managers and, uh, they don't really understand the commercial opportunities as well as they should. So I've got a job to do in lobbying them for sure. Interesting observation. Thank you. Um, oh, I have a question actually. Um, so has there been any time where you felt the system in New Zealand worked better than what you, um, see it working now?

Well, I, I think we were, we were probably had the, the system was more appropriate from a global perspective 20 years ago than it is now. I think that the big changes are changes in technology and, uh, and I think that we have to avoid building a grand edifice with that backward looking mindset and think about how we can skin this one most efficiently deliver outcomes for industries as well as delivering biosecurity.
